responses.text: The target article’s central claim is that Hawaiian pizza was invented by Greek-born Canadian restaurateur Sam Panopoulos in Chatham, Ontario, in 1962, not in Hawaii.

That core claim is strongly corroborated by BBC reporting based on a 2017 interview with Panopoulos, in which he said he and his brother experimented with canned pineapple at the Satellite Restaurant in Chatham and later put the pizza on the menu.

CBC similarly reports that Panopoulos, born in Greece and later based in Ontario, is credited with inventing Hawaiian pizza in Chatham and added pineapple with ham and bacon in 1962 after studying pizza-making in Windsor.

The Canadian Encyclopedia also says Hawaiian pizza is widely attributed to Sotirios “Sam” Panopoulos and describes the same Chatham, Ontario, Satellite Restaurant origin story, including the influence of Americanized Chinese sweet-and-savoury flavors.

Earlier reporting by the Village Voice shows that the Panopoulos origin story was investigated in 2010 and corrected details from Wikipedia, with Panopoulos saying the creation happened at the Satellite Restaurant rather than the Family Circle Restaurant.

A London Free Press obituary article quotes Panopoulos calling the pineapple pizza “an invention by accident” and places the episode at the Satellite restaurant in Chatham in the 1960s, further supporting the article’s narrative.
The article’s contextual point that Hawaii had recently become a U.S. state is accurate, as the U.S. Senate timeline says Hawaii became the 50th state by presidential proclamation on August 21, 1959.
There is some uncertainty because the invention claim relies heavily on Panopoulos’s later recollections and media interviews rather than contemporaneous 1962 documentation of the first pizza.

The main competing-origin caveat is that The Canadian Encyclopedia notes other claims and precursors, including Germany’s Toast Hawaii and Ann Arbor’s Pizza Bob’s, but it says Pizza Bob’s first advertised Hawaiian pizza only in 1971, after Panopoulos’s claimed 1962 date.

Overall, the Atlas Obscura article is credible on its main factual framing, though readers should treat the exact first-in-the-world wording as well supported by later consensus and interviews rather than proven by a surviving contemporaneous record.
